verses.text: Then the scribes and Pharisees from Jerusalem came to Jesus, saying:
“Why do your disciples break the tradition of the elders? They do not wash their hands before they eat.”
He answered, “And why do you break the commandment of God for the sake of your tradition?”
For God said, ‘Honor your father and mother,’ and, ‘Whoever curses father or mother must be put to death.’
But you say, ‘If anyone declares to their parents, “Whatever support you might have received from me is a gift devoted to God,”
they are not obligated to honor their father or mother.’ You have nullified the word of God for the sake of your tradition.
Hypocrites! Isaiah rightly prophesied about you, saying:
‘This people honors me with their lips, but their heart is far from me.
In vain do they worship me, teaching as doctrines the commandments of men.’
He called the crowd and said, “Listen and understand:
It is not what goes into the mouth that defiles a person; but what comes out of the mouth;this defiles a person.”
Then his disciples came and said, “Do you know the Pharisees were offended by this statement?”
He answered, “Every plant my Father has not planted will be uprooted.”
“Leave them alone. They are blind guides leading the blind, and if the blind lead the blind, both will fall into a pit.”
Then Peter said, “Explain this parable to us.”
Jesus said, “Are you also still without understanding?”
“Do you not see that whatever enters the mouth goes into the stomach and is expelled?”
“But what comes out of the mouth comes from the heart, and this defiles a person.”
“For out of the heart come evil thoughts, murder, adultery, sexual immorality, theft, false witness, and slander.”
“These are what defile a person, but eating with unwashed hands does not defile a person.”

sections.headline: The Faith of a Canaanite Woman

verses.text: Jesus left there and went to the region of Tyre and Sidon.
A Canaanite woman from that region came out, crying, “Lord, Son of David, have mercy on me! My daughter is severely tormented by a demon.”
But he did not answer her. His disciples urged him, “Send her away, for she keeps crying out after us.”
He replied, “I was sent only to the lost sheep of the house of Israel.”
But she came and knelt before him, saying, “Lord, help me!”
He replied, “It is not fitting to take the children's bread and throw it to the dogs.”
“Yes, Lord,” she said, “but even the dogs eat the crumbs that fall from their masters' table.”
Then Jesus said, “Dear woman, great is your faith! Let it be done for you as you desire.” And her daughter was healed from that very hour.
Jesus walked along the Sea of Galilee, went up the mountain, and sat down.
Great crowds came to him, bringing the lame, the blind, the crippled, the mute, and many others. They laid them at his feet, and he healed them.
The crowd marveled as they saw the mute speaking, the crippled made whole, the lame walking, and the blind seeing. And they praised the God of Israel.

Then Jesus called his disciples and said, “I have compassion for the crowd, because they have already been with me three days and have nothing to eat. I do not want to send them away hungry, lest they collapse on the way.”
His disciples said, “Where can we get enough bread in this desolate place to feed such a crowd?”
Jesus asked, “How many loaves do you have?” They replied, “Seven, and a few small fish.”
He instructed the crowd to sit on the ground.
Taking the seven loaves and the fish, he gave thanks, broke them, and gave them to the disciples, who shared them with the crowd.
They all ate and were satisfied, and the disciples collected seven full baskets of leftovers.
Those who ate were four thousand men, besides women and children.
After sending the crowd away, he got into the boat and went to the region of Magadan.

footnotes.content: Jesus rebukes them for using human traditions—like the “Corban” vow—to nullify God's clear command to honor and care for one's parents. By declaring resources “devoted to God,” they avoided supporting their parents while still appearing righteous. His charge exposes how pious-sounding customs were weaponized to override divine law.

“Commandments of men” refers to teachings created by altering God's actual commandments—either by changing the words or redefining their meaning. These man-made rules were treated as divine truth, but Jesus declares such worship empty.

The phrase “household dogs” translates the Greek word kynaria, a diminutive form of kyōn (“dog”), which refers to small, domestic animals kept in the home—not wild scavengers. In Jewish culture, “dogs” often symbolized Gentiles as outsiders to the covenant. Jesus' use of the softened term reflects a testing tone rather than an insult. By framing Gentile access as indirect (“crumbs”), He both affirms Israel's covenant priority and opens space for persistent faith to be honored. Her response reveals spiritual insight and humility, and Jesus' approval marks her as a forerunner of Gentile inclusion in the kingdom.
